Myles From Home

No label. No middle-man.
Jackson Myles
Every song, every move, every letter straight from him.
Come inside → Hear the music
Chapter One

The kid who taught himself

Nobody handed Jackson a blueprint. No studio, no manager, no cosign. He learned to move by watching, repeating, failing on camera, and posting anyway. Somewhere in those thousands of reps a move showed up that nobody had seen before. People called it the Jaywalk. The internet did what the internet does with something it cannot explain: it copied it, everywhere, forever.

Overnight, a Charlotte kid had the attention of millions. 1.6 million people followed a body that could do something theirs could not. That is where most stories peak and stall. His was only starting.

the belief was on a thousand before anybody knew his name
A move so simple people copied it, so strange they never forgot it.
On the Jaywalk
Chapter Two

The world, and what it costs

The attention opened rooms most people only see in magazines. He moved through fashion, through Virgil, through Dior and Louis Vuitton, through Paris and Abu Dhabi. He saw the money and the material things, the version of success everyone is told to want.

And somewhere inside all of it, the thing that was supposed to fill him did not. He had been an atheist, then agnostic, then spiritual, then Buddhist. He had been all the way around the world and all the way around himself, chasing a feeling that kept moving one room further ahead.

seen a good bit of money, material things, and still felt the hole
"I went from atheist, to agnostic, to spiritual, to Buddhist, been all around the world, seen a good bit of money and material things, just to finally understand that Jesus is all you'll ever need."
Jackson, the day he got baptized · Psalm 149:3 · 12,400 likes
Chapter Three

Home

So he came home. Back to Charlotte, back to the water, back to the start. He got baptized and said the quiet part out loud in front of everyone who had only ever watched him dance. It was not a career move. It was the opposite of one. It was a man deciding the art would mean something now, or it would not be worth making.

What comes next is the rebuild. Same body, same city, new direction. Dance got the attention. Faith gave it a reason. The music, the movement, the message, all of it points home now. And for the first time, he owns it.

love always wins
